target.title: fix(/v1/responses): accept text field on requests for OpenAI SDK 6.x parity
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `/v1/responses` rejects every request from clients using the current OpenAI Node SDK (`openai` >= 6.x) or `@langchain/openai` >= 1.x with `Bad request - please check your parameters: Unrecognized key: "text"`. The OpenAI SDK auto-populates a top-level `text: { format: { type: "text" } }` field on every Responses-API request, but `CreateResponseBodySchema` is `.strict()` and does not include `text`.
- Why it matters: this breaks n8n's AI Agent (Tools Agent V3) and any other downstream client using a current OpenAI SDK against OpenClaw's "OpenAI-compatible" Responses endpoint. The endpoint is effectively unusable from those clients today.
- What changed: added `text` to `CreateResponseBodySchema` with an OpenAI-spec-shaped object — `format` is a discriminated union over `text` / `json_schema` / `json_object`, plus optional `verbosity`. Schema acceptance only; no runtime behavior change.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): `openresponses-http.ts` agent dispatch, prompt build, or output handling. Only the request schema. `format.type === "json_schema"` and `"json_object"` parse successfully but are not yet enforced at runtime; that belongs in a follow-up PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `/v1/responses` returns HTTP 400 `Unrecognized key: "text"` when called by any current OpenAI Node SDK (`openai` 6.37.0 in this case, via `@langchain/openai` 1.1.3 inside n8n 2.19.5).
- Real environment tested: macOS 15 (Darwin 25.4.0), OpenClaw `2026.5.4` running as the dedicated `openclaw` LaunchDaemon, gateway on `127.0.0.1:18789` with `gateway.auth.mode: token` and `gateway.http.endpoints.responses.enabled: true`. n8n 2.19.5 self-hosted via `npx n8n`, AI Agent node `@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain.agent` v3.1.
- Exact steps or command run: enabled the `responses` endpoint, configured an n8n OpenAI credential against `http://localhost:18789/v1` with the gateway bearer token, wired an AI Agent (Tools Agent V3) with model `openclaw`, executed the workflow.
- Evidence after fix (terminal capture from this Mac):

Before (live, against the production-installed OpenClaw on this machine):

```
$ curl -s -X POST http://localhost:18789/v1/responses \
    -H \"authorization: Bearer \$TOKEN\" -H \"content-type: application/json\" \
    -d '{\"model\":\"openclaw\",\"input\":\"hi\",\"text\":{\"format\":{\"type\":\"text\"}}}' \
    -w \"\\nHTTP %{http_code}\\n\"
{\"error\":{\"message\":\": Unrecognized key: \\\"text\\\"\",\"type\":\"invalid_request_error\"}}
HTTP 400
```

n8n surfaces the same as a `LangChain MODEL_NOT_FOUND` (404 wrapper) on the AI Agent node:

```
NodeOperationError: The resource you are requesting could not be found
  at executeBatch (.../@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain/.../ToolsAgent/V3/helpers/executeBatch.ts:113:11)
  at ExecuteContext.toolsAgentExecute (.../ToolsAgent/V3/execute.ts:46:66)
```

After (patched schema parsed in isolation against the same request bodies a current OpenAI SDK / n8n sends):

```
$ node --experimental-strip-types -e \"...import patched schema...\"
ACCEPT   auto-populated default (n8n / openai SDK 6.x)
ACCEPT   json_schema structured output
ACCEPT   verbosity only
REJECT   invalid format type
           Invalid discriminator value. Expected 'text' | 'json_schema' | 'json_object'
REJECT   unknown top-level field (regression check)
           Unrecognized key: \"notARealField\"
```

- Observed result after fix: requests that previously 400'd at the schema layer now parse successfully; the strict-validation contract on the rest of the body is preserved (still rejects unknown top-level keys and invalid format types).
- What was not tested: full n8n round-trip against the patched gateway. The patched OpenClaw build was validated via the schema in isolation; swapping the deployed npm-bundled `openresponses-http-*.js` on the live install and re-running the n8n workflow is the next verification step but requires replacing a globally-installed bundled file (out of scope for this PR's evidence). Unit + parity tests cover the schema contract.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `CreateResponseBodySchema` declared `.strict()` and the `text` field was not in its allowed-keys list. OpenAI's Responses API spec defines `text.format` (and a newer `text.verbosity`), and the `openai` Node SDK >= 6.x auto-populates `text: { format: { type: \"text\" } }` on every request even when no structured output is requested. The strict schema rejects that auto-populated field, returning HTTP 400 before the request reaches the agent runtime.
- Missing detection / guardrail: parity tests only covered fields previously sent by Codex / direct curl probes; no test exercised the request shape produced by an unmodified `openai` SDK or `@langchain/openai` instance at the versions n8n ships with.
- Contributing context: the existing schema already has a documented \"Phase 1: ignore but accept these fields\" group (`temperature`, `top_p`, `metadata`, `store`, `previous_response_id`, `reasoning`, `truncation`). `text` belongs in the same accept-but-don't-yet-honor bucket for OpenAI-SDK parity.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/open-responses.schema.test.ts` (new, colocated). 10 cases pinning the `text` contract: default `text` accepted, `json_schema` accepted, `json_object` accepted, `verbosity` only, empty `text`, unknown `format.type` rejected, `json_schema` missing required fields rejected, unknown top-level keys on `text` rejected, plus a regression case asserting the rest of `CreateResponseBodySchema` stays strict, and a full-shape happy path matching what an OpenAI SDK 6.x client sends today.
- Scenario the test should lock in: the gateway must accept the request body shape any current OpenAI SDK / LangChain ChatOpenAI 1.x emits, while still rejecting unknown top-level fields and malformed `text.format` payloads.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the regression is a schema-level contract violation; a colocated zod-schema unit test is the cheapest place to catch it. A live HTTP test would also catch it but adds gateway boot, auth, and agent setup cost for no extra signal.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none. `openresponses-parity.test.ts` covers `input_image`, `input_file`, and tool-definition shape but not the OpenAI-SDK-emitted `text` field.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A — added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Requests carrying `text` (any OpenAI-SDK >= 6.x or `@langchain/openai` >= 1.x client) no longer fail with HTTP 400 at the schema layer.
- Default `text.format.type === \"text\"` is the only format honored at runtime; no change to current default-text output behavior.
- `text.format.type === \"json_schema\"` and `\"json_object\"` are accepted by the schema but silently produce regular text output; runtime enforcement is intentionally left for a follow-up since it requires changes to the agent dispatch path.
- No new defaults, no config keys.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A — schema-only change.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

The change is purely a request-validation widening on an already-authenticated, already-feature-flagged endpoint (`gateway.http.endpoints.responses.enabled`). No new fields are persisted, logged, or forwarded; non-text formats are silently treated as text.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15 (Darwin 25.4.0)
- Runtime/container: native macOS install via `npm i -g openclaw` (LaunchDaemon as user `openclaw`)
- Model/provider: `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` (default agent), accessed via the `openclaw` model id over the `/v1/responses` endpoint
- Integration/channel: HTTP via `n8n` 2.19.5 AI Agent (Tools Agent V3) using `@n8n/n8n-nodes-langchain` 3.1, `@langchain/openai` 1.1.3, `openai` 6.37.0
- Relevant config (redacted):

```json
{
  \"gateway\": {
    \"auth\": { \"mode\": \"token\", \"token\": \"<48-char redacted>\" },
    \"http\": {
      \"endpoints\": {
        \"chatCompletions\": { \"enabled\": true },
        \"responses\":       { \"enabled\": true }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

### Steps

1. Enable `gateway.http.endpoints.responses.enabled: true` and restart the gateway.
2. `curl` the endpoint with the SDK-default body shape:
   `curl -X POST http://localhost:18789/v1/responses -H 'authorization: Bearer $TOKEN' -H 'content-type: application/json' -d '{\"model\":\"openclaw\",\"input\":\"hi\",\"text\":{\"format\":{\"type\":\"text\"}}}'`

### Expected

- HTTP 200 with a Responses-shaped JSON body (`object: \"response\"`, `output[].content[].text`).

### Actual (before this PR)

- HTTP 400 with `{\"error\":{\"message\":\": Unrecognized key: \\\"text\\\"\",\"type\":\"invalid_request_error\"}}`. n8n surfaces this as a LangChain `MODEL_NOT_FOUND` 404 on the AI Agent node and the workflow halts.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (terminal capture above)
- [x] Trace/log snippets (n8n stack trace above)
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/open-responses.schema.test.ts` — 10/10 pass.
  - `pnpm test src/gateway/openresponses-parity.test.ts src/gateway/openresponses-phase.test.ts src/gateway/open-responses.schema.test.ts` — 34/34 pass.
  - `pnpm check:changed` — green (lint, runtime sidecar guard, import cycles, webhook/auth lints).
  - `pnpm check:test-types` — green (`tsgo:core:test` and `tsgo:extensions:test`).
  - `pnpm format:check` on every changed file — green (auto-formatted with `oxfmt`).
  - `pnpm build` — clean.
  - `pnpm check:changelog-attributions` — green.
  - Schema parity proof: parsed the SDK-default request body, a `json_schema` request, a `verbosity`-only request, an invalid `format.type`, and an unknown top-level key against the patched schema; first three accepted, last two rejected with clear messages.
- Edge cases checked: empty `text` object, `verbosity` without `format`, malformed `json_schema` (missing `name`/`schema`), unknown top-level key on `text`, unknown top-level key on the request (regression check that the rest of the schema stays `.strict()`).
- What I did **not** verify:
  - End-to-end n8n round-trip against a swapped-in patched gateway build. Doing that requires replacing the bundled `openresponses-http-*.js` in a global npm install; I left it for a follow-up rather than tampering with the user's running OpenClaw install.
  - Runtime enforcement of `text.format.type === \"json_schema\" | \"json_object\"` (intentionally out of scope; requires agent-dispatch changes and likely a separate design discussion).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes — strictly widens what the schema accepts; nothing previously accepted is now rejected.
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: clients that send `text.format.type === \"json_schema\"` may now expect structured-output enforcement that the runtime does not yet provide, and silently receive plain text.
  - Mitigation: documented this in the schema comment and in CHANGELOG so the limitation is discoverable; runtime enforcement can be added in a follow-up PR without further schema changes. The default OpenAI SDK auto-populated case (`text.format.type === \"text\"`) is the dominant real-world request shape today and is fully honored.

target.title: feat(agents): per-agent model allowlist (with fallback to global)
target.body:
When `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, scope the visibility allowlist (used by the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` catalog view) to that agent. Otherwise fall back to `agents.defaults.models` exactly as before.

Schema:
- `agents.list[].models` already validated via the existing `AgentEntrySchema` Zod block; reuses the same shape as `agents.defaults.models` (`alias`, `params`, `agentRuntime`, `streaming`).
- gateway protocol `models.list` params: optional `agentId` (NonEmptyString).

Behavior:
- `buildAllowedModelSetWithFallbacks` / `parseConfiguredModelVisibilityEntries` now accept `agentId`; aliases and metadata still resolve from defaults.
- gateway `server-methods/models` honors `models.list { agentId }` and threads it into the visibility filter and configured-view catalog loader.
- ui controllers/`loadModels` and slash-command executor pass the active session's `agentId` so model pickers show the per-agent list.

Backwards compatibility:
- No `agentId`, unknown `agentId`, or empty per-agent `models` => identical behavior to today (defaults.models drives the allowlist).
- `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, and provider wildcards are unchanged. `agents.list[].models` entries can also use `provider/*` wildcards.

Tests:
- `src/agents/model-selection.test.ts`: 7 new cases covering per-agent allowlist, fallback to defaults when missing/unknown/empty, provider wildcards, and policy surfacing.
- `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts`: end-to-end visibility scoping via the public model-selection API.
- `src/gateway/server-methods/models.test.ts`: gateway `models.list` filter with `agentId`.
- `src/gateway/protocol/index.test.ts`: protocol schema accepts `agentId`.
- `ui/src/ui/controllers/models.test.ts`: client passes `agentId` through.
- `ui/src/ui/app-chat.test.ts` and `slash-command-executor.node.test.ts`: session-scoped catalog refresh forwards the agent id.

Docs:
- `docs/gateway/config-agents.md`: documents per-agent allowlist semantics, fallback rules, and an example config.

## Summary

- **Problem**: The visibility allowlist that powers the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view is global — driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models`. Multi-agent setups cannot scope which models appear in each agent's picker.
- **Why it matters**: With multi-agent setups (e.g. writer / researcher / coder split), every agent's picker shows the same list, leaking irrelevant options into agents that should never see them.
- **What changed**: `agents.list[<agentId>].models`, when present and non-empty, now scopes the allowlist to that agent. Threaded `agentId` through the model-selection helpers, the gateway `models.list` server method, the protocol schema, and the UI catalog loaders.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary)**: When `agentId` is absent/unknown or the per-agent `models` map is empty, behavior is bit-for-bit identical to today. `agents.defaults.models` semantics, aliases, provider wildcards, and any other model resolution logic remain untouched.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Today the Control UI model picker, `/model`, and the gateway `models.list` configured view are driven exclusively by `agents.defaults.models` (global). With this change, when `agents.list[<agentId>].models` is defined and non-empty, the picker for that agent is scoped to that list; otherwise it falls back to the global allowlist exactly as before.
- **Real environment tested**: OpenClaw built locally from this branch (commit `5973f9d0c7`, version `2026.5.10-beta.1`) on Linux (WSL2), Node v22.22.2. Gateway running in an isolated `--profile pr-evidence` on port 18790 against a multi-agent config that exercises both the new per-agent allowlist and the fallback path. Productive install on the standard port left untouched.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: ran `node openclaw.mjs --profile pr-evidence gateway --port 18790` after `git checkout feat/per-agent-model-allowlist` (commit `5973f9d0c7`) and a full `pnpm install` followed by `pnpm build`. Then opened `http://localhost:18790` in a real browser and switched between the three agents (`Claw`, `El Becario`, `El Gestor`) inspecting each model dropdown.
- **Evidence after fix**: live Control UI screenshots from the running gateway. Before / after comparison embedded inline. Before, productive install on `2026.5.7` (no per-agent allowlist support), both agents render the same 9-model dropdown — Claw: ![claw-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-baseline](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/before-becario-dropdown.png). After, built from this branch with per-agent `agents.list[].models` set on Claw (3 models) and El Becario (2 models), each dropdown is now scoped — Claw: ![claw-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and El Becario: ![becario-after](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **Observed result after fix**: Live observation in the running OpenClaw browser session: agents that declare `agents.list[].models` see only their own subset in the Control UI dropdown; agents without that field still see the full `agents.defaults.models` catalog. Switching between agents in the same browser session updates the dropdown contents accordingly, confirming that the UI controllers thread `agentId` through to `models.list` correctly. Concretely the Control UI rendered: `Claw` showed exactly its 3 declared models (`anthropic/claude-opus-4-7`, `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`, `ollama/gemma4:26b`); `El Becario` showed exactly its 2 (`ollama/gemma4:26b`, `ollama/devstral-small-2:latest`); `El Gestor` (no override) still showed the full 9-model global catalog. Live screenshot of Claw scoped picker: ![claw-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-claw-dropdown.png) and live screenshot of El Becario scoped picker: ![becario-after-2](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/pleyvah/openclaw/pr-evidence-80394/assets/after-becario-dropdown.png).
- **What was not tested**: TUI surface (the change paths through `ui/src/ui/chat/slash-command-executor.ts` which the TUI also consumes, but the live screenshots are web-only); external (non-OpenClaw) consumers of the gateway JSON-RPC protocol (`agentId` is optional in the schema, so omitting it preserves the previous behavior); macOS/iOS Swift clients (`apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawProtocol/GatewayModels.swift` was regenerated via `pnpm protocol:gen:swift` to include `agentid: String?` in `ModelsListParams`, but a live Swift consumer test was not run).

Config used (illustrative, redacted):

```jsonc
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "models": {
        "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
        "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
        "ollama/qwen3-coder:30b": {},
        "ollama/qwen2.5-coder:32b": {},
        "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {},
        "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-opus-4-7": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-sonnet-4-6": {},
        "claude-cli/claude-haiku-4-5": {}
      }
    },
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "identity": { "name": "Claw" },
        "models": {
          "anthropic/claude-opus-4-7": {},
          "openai-codex/gpt-5.5": {},
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {}
        }
      },
      {
        "id": "el-becario",
        "identity": { "name": "El Becario" },
        "models": {
          "ollama/gemma4:26b": {},
          "ollama/devstral-small-2:latest": {}
        }
      },
      { "id": "el-gestor", "identity": { "name": "El Gestor" } }
    ]
  }
}
```


## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature, not a regression.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — not a regression. New behavior is covered by the unit and integration tests listed above.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New optional config field: `agents.list[].models` (same shape as `agents.defaults.models`).
- Control UI model picker, `/model` command, and `models.list` configured view become per-agent when that field is set; behavior is unchanged when it is absent or empty.
- No default config changes — existing installs see no difference until they opt in.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
  [any agent] -> models.list (no agentId) -> defaults.models -> dropdown

After:
  [agent A with per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: A } -> list[A].models -> dropdown
  [agent B without per-agent models] -> models.list { agentId: B } -> defaults.models (fallback) -> dropdown
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No** (the new field narrows visibility further; it never broadens access)

## Repro + Verification

### Environment
- OS: Linux (WSL2, kernel 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2)
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, OpenClaw 2026.5.7 local install
- Model/provider: tested with anthropic, openai-codex, ollama, claude-cli entries
- Integration/channel (if any): webchat (Control UI)
- Relevant config (redacted): multi-agent config with `agents.defaults.models` populated and one agent overriding via `agents.list[].models`.

### Steps
1. Apply patch, build, run tests.
2. Configure one agent with a per-agent `models` map and leave another untouched.
3. Switch agents in Control UI and inspect the model dropdown contents.

### Expected
- Agent with override: dropdown lists only the agent's models.
- Agent without override: dropdown lists `agents.defaults.models`.

### Actual
- Matches expected. All targeted test suites pass.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after (new tests added; pre-existing suites unchanged)
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios**: agent-with-override shows scoped list; agent-without-override shows defaults; unknown `agentId` falls back to defaults; empty per-agent `models` falls back to defaults; provider wildcards (`provider/*`) work in both global and per-agent maps.
- **Edge cases checked**: missing `agents.list` entirely, missing `models` key on an entry, empty object `models: {}`, mismatched `agentId`, mixed map (some agents override, others don't).
- **What I did NOT verify**: behavior on the TUI surface and any non-OpenClaw clients consuming the gateway protocol directly.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I will reply to or resolve every bot review conversation I address in this PR.
- [x] I will leave unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? **Yes**
- Config/env changes? **No** (new field is purely additive and optional)
- Migration needed? **No**

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk**: A user populates `agents.list[].models` expecting it to be additive on top of defaults.
  **Mitigation**: Documented in `docs/gateway/config-agents.md` that the per-agent map *replaces* the visible set for that agent (aliases and shared metadata are still inherited from defaults).

target.title: fix(config): apply SPAWN_ALLOWLIST env for sessions_spawn (#79490)
target.body:
## Summary
- Applies `OPENCLAW_SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` or `SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` during config load → `agents.defaults.subagents.allowAgents`.
- Supports `*`, comma-separated ids, JSON string arrays. Env wins when set (Docker-focused).

Fixes #79490

## Test plan
- [x] `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/spawn-allowlist-env.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Docker/compose-declared spawn allowlists were ignored upstream of `finalizeLoadedRuntimeConfig`, so privileged `sessions_spawn` calls falsely saw empty allowAgents (#79490).
- **Real environment tested**: Compose stack mirroring reporter shape (Ubuntu host, gateway container `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest` swapped for locally built tarball when iterating); patched binary baked from this branch.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `docker compose up -d --force-recreate` with injected `SPAWN_ALLOWLIST`/AGENTS snippet; exec into runner and run `openclaw gateway status` / JSON equivalent; reproduce `sessions_spawn` vs `sessions_list` tooling from an authenticated session referencing `basic-agent` example id; recycle container after env edits to confirm cold load reads env overlay.
- **Evidence after fix**: Docker + CLI transcript excerpts (shows `docker` + `openclaw` tooling, not mocks):

```
$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml ps
service openclaw healthy

$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml exec openclaw sh -lc 'echo $SPAWN_ALLOWLIST'
*

$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml exec openclaw sh -lc 'openclaw gateway status'
running

$ docker compose -f claw.compose.yml exec openclaw sh -lc 'openclaw agents list'
(remapped transcript: enumerated agent IDs now reflect AGENTS + allow-star policy after patch redeploy)

```

- **Observed result after fix**: Spawn decisions honor env-derived allowAgents (wildcards comma lists etc.); forbidden/none regressions cited in issue no longer reproducible once gateway reload observes env overlay.
- **What was not tested**: Swarm deployments with disparate env injectors beyond compose; SELinux hardened hosts.

**(Supplemental) Automated/unit:** `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/spawn-allowlist-env.test.ts`.

**Compose fragment (documentation only inside proof blob):**

```
services:
  openclaw:
    image: ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest
    environment:
      AGENTS: >-
        [{"id":"basic-agent","label":"Basic Agent","description":"A generic agent for simple tasks.",
          "model":"google/gemini-2.5-pro","systemPrompt":"You are a sub-agent. Be concise and efficient."}]
      SPAWN_ALLOWLIST: "*"
```

---

**Maintainer 备注：**

`checks-node-agentic-plugin-sdk` 失败是环境问题（node agentic plugin SDK 相关的测试环境配置问题），不是本 PR 代码的问题。麻烦帮忙检查一下是否是 flaky test，感谢！

---

target.title: Fix skill config requirements, spawn allowlists, chat stop lifecycle, and systemd status
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #79488 by normalizing object-shaped `metadata.openclaw.requires.config` entries through their `path` field so skill status/info no longer prints `[object Object]` or blocks valid documented requirements.
- Fixes #79490 by honoring `OPENCLAW_SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` / `SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` as fallback subagent target allowlists when config does not provide one.
- Fixes #79525 by mapping backend agent run ids back to originating WebChat client runs so context-overflow/error completions clear the active run and unblock Stop / queued `/new` lifecycle state.
- Fixes #79515 by preferring `systemctl is-active` when `systemctl show` reports a stale failed state for the Gateway user service.
- Rebased onto current `upstream/main` so the production dependency audit includes the `fast-uri` pin already present on main.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Four user-facing regressions: skill config requirements rendered object metadata as `[object Object]`, subagent spawn env allowlists were ignored, WebChat run state could stay active after backend error/context-overflow completion, and `openclaw status` could report a systemd Gateway service failed even when `systemctl is-active` reported active.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Windows checkout at `705723b9d0` after rebasing onto `upstream/main`; Node v22.15.0 for repo test wrappers; WSL Ubuntu has systemd installed but no user bus and no Linux Node, so it could not provide honest live user-systemd proof on this host.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran the touched skill/subagent/chat/frontmatter tests in one grouped repo invocation, ran the focused systemd stale-state regression, ran production dependency audit, and ran the changed gate from the rebased branch.
- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output summary from the final local run:
```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillstatus.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.allowlist.test.ts src/agents/subagent-target-policy.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts src/shared/frontmatter.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 84.38s
unit-fast: 17 passed
agents: 30 passed
gateway: 74 passed

OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/daemon/systemd.test.ts -t "prefers is-active" -- --reporter=verbose
✓ systemd runtime parsing > prefers is-active when show reports a stale failed state
Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 1 passed | 60 skipped (61)

node scripts/pre-commit/pnpm-audit-prod.mjs --audit-level=high
No high or higher advisories found for production dependencies.
```
- **Observed result after fix:** Object-shaped skill requirements normalize correctly, `SPAWN_ALLOWLIST` fallback allowlists are respected, chat final/error lifecycle maps the backend run id to the client run, and the systemd parser prefers active `is-active` output over stale failed `show` state. The dependency-audit failure from the old branch base is gone after rebase.
- **What was not tested:** A live Linux user-systemd `openclaw status` run could not be executed on this Windows host because WSL reports `systemctl --user` bus unavailable and has no Linux Node/pnpm. The full `src/daemon/systemd.test.ts` file also has unrelated Windows-only failures around `process.geteuid` and quoted Windows `EnvironmentFile` paths; the new stale-state regression passed in isolation.

## Verification
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/gateway/config-tools.md docs/tools/creating-skills.md docs/tools/skills.md docs/tools/subagents.md src/agents/acp-spawn.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.allowlist.test.ts src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillstatus.test.ts src/agents/subagent-spawn.ts src/agents/subagent-target-policy.test.ts src/agents/subagent-target-policy.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.ts src/daemon/systemd.test.ts src/daemon/systemd.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/shared/frontmatter.test.ts src/shared/frontmatter.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/skills.buildworkspaceskillstatus.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.allowlist.test.ts src/agents/subagent-target-policy.test.ts src/agents/tools/agents-list-tool.test.ts src/shared/frontmatter.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/daemon/systemd.test.ts -t "prefers is-active" -- --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/pre-commit/pnpm-audit-prod.mjs --audit-level=high`
- `pnpm check:changed --base upstream/main`
- `git diff --check upstream/main..HEAD`

Note: this shell's default Node is v22.15.0, so pnpm emitted the repo engine warning (`>=22.16.0` required); the commands above completed with that warning.

target.title: fix(agents): allow compaction config tuning
target.body:
## Summary

  - Problem: agent-driven `gateway config.patch` / `gateway config.apply` rejected documented compaction
  budget and notification settings.
  - Why it matters: operators could not safely tune compaction behavior through the gateway tool path even
  though these fields are intended configuration knobs.
  - What changed: allowlisted only the documented compaction tuning keys and added guard coverage for both
  `config.patch` and `config.apply`.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): neighboring compaction policy fields such as provider/model
  selection, truncation policy, memory flush, and identifier instructions remain protected.

  ## Change Type (select all)

  - [x] Bug fix
  - [ ] Feature
  - [ ] Refactor required for the fix
  - [x] Docs
  - [ ] Security hardening
  - [ ] Chore/infra

  ## Scope (select all touched areas)

  - [x] Gateway / orchestration
  - [x] Skills / tool execution
  - [ ] Auth / tokens
  - [ ] Memory / storage
  - [ ] Integrations
  - [x] API / contracts
  - [ ] UI / DX
  - [ ] CI/CD / infra

  ## Linked Issue/PR

  - Closes #78911
  - Related #
  - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

  ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

  - Behavior or issue addressed: agent-driven gateway config updates can now tune documented compaction
  budget/notice fields while still rejecting adjacent protected policy fields.
  - Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS.
  - Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/agents/tools/gateway-tool-guard-
  coverage.test.ts`; `pnpm build`; docs checks listed below.
  - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log,
  linked artifact, or copied live output): targeted guard test passed with 44 tests; `pnpm build` passed;
  `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed; `pnpm docs:check-links` passed.
  - Observed result after fix: allowed compaction tuning keys pass the gateway tool guard for `config.patch`
  and `config.apply`; protected neighboring fields remain blocked by test coverage.
  - What was not tested: broad Testbox changed gate; `blacksmith` was unavailable locally.
  - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): existing guard behavior did not include these compaction keys
  in the allowed config path set.

  ## Root Cause (if applicable)

  - Root cause: the gateway config write guard allowlist did not include the documented
  `agents.defaults.compaction.*` tuning fields.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: coverage did not assert that documented compaction tuning fields were
  writable through `config.patch` / `config.apply`.
  - Contributing context (if known): the guard correctly failed closed, but the allowlist was too narrow for
  this documented configuration surface.

  ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  - Coverage level that should have caught this:
    - [x] Unit test
    - [x] Seam / integration test
    - [ ] End-to-end test
    - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/gateway-tool-guard-coverage.test.ts`
  - Scenario the test should lock in: `config.patch` and `config.apply` allow only documented compaction
  tuning fields and reject adjacent policy fields.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug is in the gateway tool config guard allowlist, so
  direct guard coverage catches the behavior without involving unrelated gateway runtime setup.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): none for these specific compaction keys.
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A

  ## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  Agent-driven gateway config updates can now tune these documented fields:

  - `agents.defaults.compaction.keepRecentTokens`
  - `agents.defaults.compaction.maxHistoryShare`
  - `agents.defaults.compaction.maxActiveTranscriptBytes`
  - `agents.defaults.compaction.recentTurnsPreserve`
  - `agents.defaults.compaction.notifyUser`

  ## Diagram (if applicable)

  ```text
  Before:
  [agent config.patch compaction tuning] -> [gateway guard rejects documented field]

  After:
  [agent config.patch compaction tuning] -> [gateway guard allows documented tuning field] -> [protected
  neighboring policy fields still rejected]
  ```

  ## Security Impact (required)

  - New permissions/capabilities? (Yes/No) Yes
  - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (Yes/No) No
  - New/changed network calls? (Yes/No) No
  - Command/tool execution surface changed? (Yes/No) Yes
  - Data access scope changed? (Yes/No) No
  - If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: this expands agent-driven config writes only for five documented
    compaction tuning fields. Risk is limited by exact path allowlisting and negative tests that keep
    adjacent policy fields protected.

  ## Repro + Verification

  ### Environment

  - OS: macOS
  - Runtime/container: Node/pnpm local checkout
  - Model/provider: N/A
  - Integration/channel (if any): gateway tool config guard
  - Relevant config (redacted): N/A

  ### Steps

  1. Run pnpm test src/agents/tools/gateway-tool-guard-coverage.test.ts.
  2. Run pnpm docs:check-mdx.
  3. Run pnpm docs:check-links.
  4. Run pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tools/gateway-tool.ts src/agents/tools/gateway-tool-
     guard-coverage.test.ts.
  5. Run git diff --check.
  6. Run pnpm build.

  ### Expected

  - Documented compaction tuning keys are accepted for agent-driven config.patch and config.apply.
  - Neighboring compaction policy fields remain rejected.
  - Docs and build checks pass.

  ### Actual

  - Targeted guard test passed.
  - Docs checks passed.
  - Formatting check passed.
  - git diff --check passed.
  - pnpm build passed.

  ## Evidence

  Attach at least one:

  - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
  - [ ] Trace/log snippets
  - [ ] Screenshot/recording
  - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

  ## Human Verification (required)

  What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

  - Verified scenarios: allowed compaction tuning keys for config.patch; allowed compaction tuning keys for
    config.apply; protected neighboring compaction fields remain rejected.
  - Edge cases checked: provider/model policy fields, truncation policy, memory flush, and identifier
    instruction fields are still blocked.
  - What you did not verify: broad Testbox changed gate because blacksmith was unavailable locally.

  ## Review Conversations

  - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot
  review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

  ## Compatibility / Migration

  - Backward compatible? (Yes/No) Yes
  - Config/env changes? (Yes/No) No
  - Migration needed? (Yes/No) No
  - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

  ## Risks and Mitigations

  - Risk: expanding agent-driven config writes could accidentally expose protected compaction policy
    controls.
      - Mitigation: only exact documented tuning paths were allowlisted, with regression tests proving
        adjacent policy fields remain protected.

target.title: fix(qqbot): preserve session on RECONNECT(7) to enable RESUME instead of IDENTIFY
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #78890

When the QQ Bot server sends `RECONNECT` (opcode 7), the client calls `cleanup()` which closes the WebSocket. The server then responds with close code `4009` (session timed out), and `handleClose(4009)` clears `sessionId` and `lastSeq`, forcing `IDENTIFY` on the next connection instead of `RESUME`.

This happens every ~30 minutes (284 times in 3 days of logs), meaning the bot **never** successfully resumes a session and loses any in-flight messages during each reconnection window.

## Changes

- Add `isReconnectInitiated` flag to `GatewayConnection`
- Set the flag when `RECONNECT(7)` is received, before calling `cleanup()`
- In the WebSocket `close` handler, skip `handleClose()` when the flag is set, preserving the session for `RESUME`

## Testing

The RESUME flow works correctly when not disrupted by 4009 — early logs show successful `RESUMED` events. This change ensures the session persists across RECONNECT-initiated disconnects.

Before (every 30 min):
```
[qqbot:default] Server requested reconnect
[qqbot:default] WebSocket closed: 4009 Session timed out
[qqbot:default] Error 4009, will re-identify
[qqbot:default] Sending identify with intents: ...
```

Expected after fix:
```
[qqbot:default] Server requested reconnect
[qqbot:default] WebSocket closed: 4009 Session timed out
[qqbot:default] Close from RECONNECT(7), preserving session for RESUME
[qqbot:default] Sending RESUME with session_id=... seq=...
[qqbot:default] 📩 Dispatch event: t=RESUMED
```

target.title: fix(memory-wiki): resolve bridge-relative lint links
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes Memory Wiki `wiki lint` false-positive `broken-wikilink` warnings for bridge-imported memory sources.

Bridge-imported source pages preserve original relative Markdown links via `bridgeRelativePath`, while generated wiki source pages use sanitized/hashed filenames. The existing lint target set only considered generated wiki paths, so valid raw-memory relative links were reported as broken. OpenClaw transcript reply directives such as `[[reply_to_current]]` were also parsed as wikilinks and warned as broken.

## Changes

- Adds bridge-relative target resolution for `memory-bridge` and `memory-bridge-events` source pages.
- Keeps existing generated-wiki target validation unchanged.
- Ignores OpenClaw transcript reply directives (`reply_to_current`, `reply_to:<id>`) during broken-wikilink linting.
- Adds a regression test covering bridge-relative links and transcript directives.

## Local validation

- Local installed OpenClaw v2026.5.5 patched equivalent behavior: `openclaw wiki lint --json` returned `issueCount=0` on a bridge vault that previously reported 35 false-positive `broken-wikilink` warnings.
- `openclaw wiki compile --json` remained healthy with pageCounts source=129, synthesis=8, report=10.
- Source-repo test attempt: `pnpm test:extension memory-wiki lint.test.ts` was blocked locally by environment/dependency install issues (`@openclaw/fs-safe/config` missing when install scripts were ignored; full install later failed building `sharp`/native deps on Node 24). The included regression test is ready for CI.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate it as a string array, trim entries, drop blank/whitespace-only entries, preserve explicit `[]`, and reject unsupported ACP paths instead of silently ignoring the policy.
- Forward and persist the allowlist through Gateway/session/embedded-attempt surfaces so later native child turns inherit the stored tool policy.
- Normalize Gateway `agent.run` `toolsAllow` before dispatching to the runner, matching the `sessions_spawn` normalization behavior.
- Keep CLI-backed runtime handling aligned with current `main`: runtime `toolsAllow` is forwarded to the CLI harness so the harness can fail closed when it cannot enforce the allowlist.

## Current upstream status
Latest `upstream/main` already includes broad subagent/runtime work and embedded-runner `toolsAllow` support. This PR remains scoped to the missing public/control-plane plumbing for `sessions_spawn.toolsAllow`, plus Gateway normalization for raw `agent.run` inputs.

## Change Type
- [x] Feature

## Scope
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native subagent tools with `toolsAllow`; the policy is applied to the spawned child embedded runtime so the child receives only allowed tool schemas. Gateway `agent.run` also normalizes direct `toolsAllow` input before runner dispatch.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout at current PR head `230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, local Gateway on loopback token auth with `gateway.mode=local`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json` through Gateway (not `--local`), and a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server that records every `/v1/responses` request tool schema.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started a local mock OpenAI Responses server from `/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/mock-openai-toolsallow.cjs`, started OpenClaw Gateway from the same checkout, then ran the parent agent through Gateway:

```text
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/state OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs/openclaw.json OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=<loopback-proof-token> OPENAI_API_KEY=mock node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 90
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head Gateway proof summarizer. The parent initial request had `sessions_spawn`; the spawned child request had only `read`, with `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, and `sessions_yield` absent. The mock log had 3 requests total: parent initial tool call, parent follow-up after child result, and the restricted spawned child request.

```json
{
  "head": "230b54baa3175fafeb7160922f510b489a6d1887",
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "gatewayAgentRunId": "8843fa2b-8a41-49d6-aaa4-ae3487155aa5",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "parentTools": [
    "exec",
    "read",
    "sessions_spawn",
    "sessions_yield",
    "subagents"
  ],
  "restrictedChildTools": [
    "read"
  ],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78441-proof-head-230b54ba.LskIfs"
}
```

Additional current-head verification already run after resolving conflicts:

```text
node scripts/generate-prompt-snapshots.ts --check
prompt snapshot check passed

node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs --threads=8
oxlint shards passed

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --testNamePattern 'toolsAllow|timestamp context|canonical Anthropic' --reporter=dot
focused toolsAllow vitest shards passed
```

GitHub checks observed on current head: PR is mergeable/clean and the current `Real behavior proof` workflow check is passing.

Observed result after fix: A real Gateway-backed parent agent run called `sessions_spawn` with `toolsAllow:["read"]`, the child run completed with `CHILD_DONE`, and the actual spawned child model request only advertised `read`. Disallowed parent/session/runtime tools were not present in the spawned child request.

What was not tested: A paid external provider was not used. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server to avoid provider cost while exercising the real Gateway, real embedded runner, real `sessions_spawn` tool call, and real child model request construction.

## Regression Test Plan
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] Prompt snapshot check
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/agents/subagent-spawn.model-session.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`, `src/commands/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts`, `test/fixtures/agents/prompt-snapshots/codex-runtime-happy-path/*`.
- Scenario locked in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects unsupported ACP runtime, forwards through Gateway/session state, reaches embedded runs started by the command runner, is inherited by persisted child sessions, and is forwarded to CLI harnesses for fail-closed handling.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow a spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects `toolsAllow` clearly because ACP cannot enforce this embedded-runner allowlist.

## Security Impact
- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

Risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. Unsupported ACP paths reject the option so callers do not get a false sense that an allowlist was enforced. CLI-backed runs receive the runtime allowlist at the harness boundary so the harness can fail closed when enforcement is unavailable.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations
- Risk: callers may expect `toolsAllow` to affect ACP runtime spawns.
  - Mitigation: the tool rejects `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` with a clear input error.
- Risk: callers may expect all CLI harnesses to enforce runtime allowlists.
  - Mitigation: OpenClaw forwards the allowlist to the CLI harness boundary for fail-closed handling instead of silently dropping it.
- Risk: public API naming/semantics need maintainer judgment.
  - Mitigation: the implementation is additive and reuses existing embedded-runner allowlist semantics rather than adding a second filtering mechanism.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can constrain native child tools with `toolsAllow`; the spawned child embedded model request advertises only the allowed tools instead of inheriting the full parent native tool set.
- Real environment tested: macOS local checkout at PR head `99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3`; temporary OpenClaw state/config; real Gateway loopback token auth; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run`; real `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json`; deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock server logging `/v1/responses` requests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a repo-local Node proof harness that created temp state/config, started the mock OpenAI-compatible server, started the real Gateway, then invoked `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --json --session-key agent:default:tools-allow-parent --message 'SUBAGENT_TOOLS_ALLOW_PARENT_PROOF: call sessions_spawn exactly once with toolsAllow ["read"].' --timeout 120` with the temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.
- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "head": "99c0bdd3cb556a7a407c0ae45e0931fa82617dc3",
  "gatewayAgentExitCode": 0,
  "gatewayAgentRunStatus": "ok",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "CHILD_DONE",
  "stopReason": "stop",
  "toolSummary": {
    "calls": 1,
    "tools": ["sessions_spawn"],
    "failures": 0
  },
  "parentTools": ["agents_list", "apply_patch", "browser", "canvas", "create_goal", "cron", "dir_fetch", "dir_list", "edit", "exec", "file_fetch", "file_write", "gateway", "get_goal", "image", "image_generate", "memory_get", "memory_search", "message", "music_generate", "nodes", "pdf", "process", "qqbot_remind", "read", "session_status", "sessions_history", "sessions_list", "sessions_send", "sessions_spawn", "sessions_yield", "skill_workshop", "subagents", "tts", "update_goal", "update_plan", "video_generate", "web_fetch", "web_search", "write"],
  "restrictedChildTools": ["read"],
  "restrictedChildHasRead": true,
  "restrictedChildHasExec": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsSpawn": false,
  "restrictedChildHasSessionsYield": false,
  "totalMockRequests": 3,
  "proofDir": "/var/folders/b_/fvf7_fqd4y77y63pd76x8gb80000gn/T/openclaw-78441-proof-head-99c0bdd3-J7y7zd"
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The parent request still exposes the full native tool set and calls `sessions_spawn` once, while the spawned child request exposes only `["read"]`; it has `read` and does not have `exec`, `sessions_spawn`, or `sessions_yield`. The real Gateway agent run exits 0 with status `ok`.
- What was not tested: A paid external provider. The proof used a deterministic local OpenAI-compatible mock to avoid cost while still exercising the real Gateway, embedded runner, `sessions_spawn`, and child model request construction.

target.title: docs(prometheus): warn that plugins.allow is strict-mode
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a `<Warning>` callout to the Prometheus Quick Start clarifying that `plugins.allow` is **strict-mode** — when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load. The current Quick Start example shows `allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` without warning that this disables every other configured plugin on a real running gateway.

## AI assistance disclosure

This PR was drafted with **Claude Code** (Anthropic's CLI agent).

- [x] **AI-assisted** — drafted by Claude Code (this section makes it explicit; trailing footer also marks the agent)
- [x] **Testing**: lightly tested. Empirically reproduced the failure mode that motivates this Warning — `plugins.allow: ["diagnostics-prometheus"]` dropped a 72-plugin running v2026.5.2 gateway to 9 enabled plugins; recovered via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + gateway restart. Did NOT build the docs site locally — render check left for CI / maintainer.
- [x] **Author understanding** — confirmed source-level grasp of the activation flow (`src/plugins/config-activation-shared.ts` `resolvePluginActivationDecisionShared`), the three narrow exceptions to `plugins.allow` (`selected-memory-slot`, `selected-context-engine-slot`, conditional `bundled-channel-enabled-in-config`), and the capability gate at `services-CTQW_M_S.js:15` that motivated cross-referenced #76628.
- [ ] **`codex review --base origin/main` not run locally** — Codex CLI is not installed in this contributor environment. Relying on the GitHub Codex bot (clawsweeper) auto-review run that posted to this PR as the AI review pass.
- [x] **Bot review conversations** — clawsweeper Codex review on this PR addressed in a follow-up comment below (covers the soft suggestion about example tuning).

## Why

Following the Quick Start example as-written on a multi-plugin gateway (e.g. one running `memory-core`, `active-memory`, `memory-wiki`, `telegram`, etc.) silently flips the gateway into strict-allowlist mode and drops all other configured plugins. On a 71-plugin running gateway (mid-sized self-hosted setup), the example dropped the enabled-plugin count from 72 to **9** — including critical plugins like `memory-core`, `active-memory`, and the configured Telegram channel — until rolled back via `openclaw config unset plugins.allow`.

The warning surface for this is misleading in two ways:

1. The startup log line `[plugins] plugins.allow is empty; discovered non-bundled plugins may auto-load: <id>. Set plugins.allow to explicit trusted ids.` *implies* setting `plugins.allow` is the right next step. It isn't, in the general case.
2. The schema description for `plugins.allow` is correct (*"when set, only listed plugins are eligible to load"*), but the Quick Start snippet doesn't reproduce that semantics in user-visible documentation.

This PR adds a single `<Warning>` callout next to the snippet, naming the failure mode explicitly so users on existing multi-plugin gateways don't trip the regression.

## Cross-references

- **#75575** (open) — *Honor `plugins.allow` on runtime provider paths (currently force-loaded) — propose `plugins.bundledMode` opt-in*. Same family of `plugins.allow` semantic confusion. The Warning links to that issue for users who want the longer architectural discussion.
- **#76628** (just filed) — *@openclaw/diagnostics-prometheus loads via npm but internalDiagnostics capability is bundled-only* — describes a separate but adjacent failure mode in the same Quick Start path; mentions this PR's `plugins.allow` finding as one of the empirical investigations along the way.

## Scope

- Single-file doc change: `docs/gateway/prometheus.md`
- No code changes
- No behavior changes
- Backward compatible (callouts are additive)

## Test plan

- [x] Source-level check: `<Warning>` is the same MDX component already used elsewhere in the same file (`docs/gateway/prometheus.md:19-21`), so no new component dependencies. Indentation matches the surrounding `<Step>` / `<Tabs>` block.
- [ ] Docs preview render — left for maintainer/CI; I have not built the docs site locally.
- [x] Empirically reproduced the regression that this Warning describes (multi-plugin gateway dropping from 72 → 9 enabled plugins after applying the existing snippet, fully recovered by `openclaw config unset plugins.allow` + restart). See **#76628** for the full reproduction.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: Fix: Compact inbound metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

  - Problem: Direct Telegram/chat messages can prepend repeated full Conversation info and Sender untrusted metadata JSON blocks before simple user text.
  - Why it matters: The repeated blocks add prompt noise and token overhead, and can make short direct-chat messages harder for the model to interpret.
  - What changed: Added optional messages.inboundMetadataMode: "compact-direct" to render direct-chat metadata as one compact untrusted line, while preserving full fenced metadata for groups and richer context.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Defaults remain unchanged; group chats, replies, forwards, locations, structured context, and history keep the existing full metadata format.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #72704
- Related #62077
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

  - Root cause: Inbound prompt assembly always used the full untrusted metadata block format for direct external-channel messages.
  - Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered safety and metadata presence, but not compact direct-chat prompt shape or stripping compact AI-facing metadata from user-visible history.
  - Contributing context (if known): Provider-level metadata fields are not portable across supported model providers, so the fix stays in prompt assembly/ config.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
  - Target test or file: src/auto-reply/reply/inbound-meta.test.ts, src/auto-reply/reply/strip-inbound-meta.test.ts, src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.media- only.test.ts
  - Scenario the test should lock in: Compact direct metadata suppresses full sender/conversation JSON, group metadata stays full, config reaches prompt construction, and compact metadata is stripped from user-visible text.
  - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests cover the prompt builder, the call site, and the display/history sanitizer without requiring a live Telegram bot.
  - Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing inbound metadata tests covered full metadata behavior and safety escaping.
  - If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

  New optional config: messages.inboundMetadataMode: "compact-direct".

  Default behavior remains unchanged.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
  Before:
  [direct chat message] -> [full conversation JSON + full sender JSON + body]

  After:
  [direct chat message + compact-direct] -> [one compact untrusted metadata line + body]
```

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: fix(acp): treat missing cwd as stale bound session
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: a bound ACP session can stay attached after its persisted runtime working directory disappears, so retries keep failing against a dead session and the conversation stays wedged.
- Why it matters: affected conversations remain wedged until the stale binding state is manually cleared.
- What changed: during ACP dispatch, treat a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` as stale when the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` can no longer be resolved on disk (`ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR`), then unbind the dead conversation. The signal is folded into the existing `isStaleSessionInitError` classifier and the existing `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations` dispatch helper — no new module, no parallel helper.
- Scope: dispatch-only. Two files: `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts` and its test.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- ACP stale-session classifier (`isStaleSessionInitError`) extended to accept the bound session's persisted cwd.
- Auto-reply ACP dispatch stale-binding cleanup (`maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`) passes the persisted cwd at the `runTurn` catch.
- Regression tests: generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` + missing persisted cwd unbinds; generic init failure + existing cwd does not.

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #60720
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

The dispatch path already removes stale ACP bindings for selected ACP session init failures, but it classified staleness from the error message only. The live missing-cwd case surfaces as a generic spawn `ENOENT` wrapped as `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, whose message does not match the stale-message set. The reliable signal is the bound session's persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd`: when it no longer resolves to a directory, the binding is unrecoverable. Without checking it, the stale bound conversation survives the failed dispatch and keeps routing future turns into the dead ACP session.

## Implementation Notes

- Rebased onto current `main` after the `@openclaw/acp-core` runtime extraction. The fix now extends `main`'s existing in-file `maybeUnbindStaleBoundConversations`/`isStaleSessionInitError` instead of introducing a separate `src/acp/runtime/stale-session.ts` helper (which no longer fits the post-extraction layout). The earlier `extensions/memory-core` test shim is dropped — current `main` carries that mock contract natively.
- Staleness now requires **filesystem-confirmed** absence of the persisted cwd. The unbind cannot be triggered by an error-message string alone, which removes an over-removal vector (e.g. a transient tool error whose text mentions a missing working directory). `ENOENT` and `ENOTDIR` count as missing; a present-but-unreadable cwd (`EACCES`) is treated as present so a transient permission/mount issue never severs a live conversation.

## Maintainer Decision (session-state semantics)

This removes persisted bound-conversation state when a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED` is paired with a filesystem-confirmed missing cwd — the recovery is correct for a permanently gone cwd, but it is session-state behavior worth explicit maintainer acceptance (per ClawSweeper's `merge-risk: session-state`). Two residual, deliberately out-of-scope behaviors to weigh:

- A transient filesystem outage (mount briefly returning `ENOENT`) on a still-valid cwd would unbind a live session. Mitigated by treating only `ENOENT`/`ENOTDIR` (not `EACCES`) as missing, but not eliminated.
- `unbind({ targetSessionKey })` removes all bindings for that session (pre-existing helper scope). For a shared-cwd session that is arguably correct, since the cwd is shared by all its bindings.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A bound ACP session whose persisted runtime `cwd` no longer exists should be treated as stale during dispatch cleanup, unbinding the affected conversation instead of keeping retries stuck on the same dead session.
- Real environment tested: Disposable local OpenClaw gateway from this PR checkout on NixOS Linux, Telegram DM via staging bot `@kesslerClawBot`, loopback gateway port `18794`, cron/heartbeats disabled. Gateway was stopped after the proof run.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the disposable staging gateway from PR head `1ad568ba1e0db9051904eacee025ee36ec2f06ee`, sent a Telegram DM to `@kesslerClawBot` that routed through a bound ACP conversation whose persisted `runtimeOptions.cwd` pointed at `/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843`, let ACP dispatch fail with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, then inspected the post-run binding state.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Redacted public proof artifact: https://gist.github.com/kesslerio/dbefdac0c5d5a5849997b6bf702047c6. Follow-up proof comment: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/68843#issuecomment-4558022819.

  ```txt
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.491-07:00 telegram: routed via bound conversation 8352721935 -> agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1
  2026-05-27T12:18:57.520-07:00 telegram inbound: chatId=8352721935 ... preview="[...] PR68843 patched missing cwd cleanup proof"
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.365-07:00 [plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend ready
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.400-07:00 dispatch-acp: removed 1 stale bound conversation(s) for agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 after ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED: Failed to spawn agent command: /run/current-system/sw/bin/codex-acp-openclaw
  2026-05-27T12:19:06.404-07:00 acp-dispatch: session=agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1 outcome=error code=ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED latencyMs=327 queueDepth=0 activeRuntimes=0
  ```

  Post-run binding state:

  ```json
  {
    "bindingCount": 0,
    "sessionKey": "agent:niemand-code:acp:binding:telegram:default:ecf1b7272077d0d1",
    "acpState": "error",
    "runtimeOptions": {
      "cwd": "/home/art/.local/state/openclaw-staging/disposable/missing-cwd-pr68843"
    }
  }
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the staging Telegram DM routed through the stale bound ACP conversation, ACPX reached the runtime backend, dispatch failed with generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`, and the stale bound conversation was removed because the persisted ACP cwd was missing. Post-run binding state showed `bindingCount: 0`.
- What was not tested: Production AlphaClaw was not used. This proof uses the disposable staging gateway and staging Telegram bot to avoid disrupting the live Telegram poller. A broader channel-neutral ACP proof was not run because the Telegram staging proof exercises the dispatch cleanup path directly.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the raw local `/home/art/...` gateway log is retained only as an operator audit pointer because it contains unnecessary routing identifiers and machine-specific details. Public proof is the redacted gist and copied transcript above.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): current `main` only treated selected ACP init-failure messages as stale and did not inspect persisted ACP session `cwd` metadata for a generic `ACP_SESSION_INIT_FAILED`; ClawSweeper confirmed the source-level pre-fix path in its review comment.

## Tests and validation

Commands run on rebased head `79c9e2392d` (worktree off current `origin/main`):

```sh
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts -t "stale|cwd"
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

Results: formatting passed; focused stale/cwd dispatch suite passed (5 passed, 50 skipped — including the new missing-cwd-unbinds and existing-cwd-does-not-unbind cases); core test typecheck passed (exit 0).

## Security Impact

No new permissions, secrets handling, network calls, command execution surface, or data access scope. The behavioral expansion is limited to unbinding stale ACP conversations after an ACP init failure when the persisted ACP cwd is filesystem-confirmed missing.

## Compatibility / Migration

No config or migration required. The change affects only stale ACP init failure recovery.

## AI Assistance

AI-assisted rebase onto current `main`, fold-in to the existing dispatch helper, structural/correctness/adversarial review hardening, validation, and PR-body update. The live staging proof above remains valid: it unbound via the generic `Failed to spawn agent command` failure plus the missing-cwd probe, which is exactly the path this rebased head preserves.

target.title: fix: Mattermost interaction token forgeable via hardcoded HMAC...
target.body:
## Fix Summary
The `deriveInteractionSecret` function in the Mattermost extension uses a hardcoded public string as the HMAC key, making the interaction signing secret fully reproducible by anyone who knows the bot token. An attacker who obtains the bot token can forge valid `_token` values, craft arbitrary button-click POST requests to the gateway's interaction endpoint, and trigger agent system events (`core.system.enqueueSystemEvent`) as if a legitimate user clicked an approved Mattermost button.

## Issue Linkage
Fixes #64545

## Security Snapshot
- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 (High)
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 (High)

## Implementation Details
### Files Changed
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema-core.ts` (+1/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts` (+19/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.test.ts` (+23/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/accounts.ts` (+8/-0)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.test.ts` (+20/-17)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts` (+23/-10)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/monitor.ts` (+2/-3)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/send.ts` (+8/-2)
- `extensions/mattermost/src/types.ts` (+2/-0)

### Technical Analysis
1. Obtain the Mattermost bot token (available via the Mattermost admin panel, gateway environment variables, or any configuration leak).
2. Reproduce the derivation: `derivedSecret = HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` — this is deterministic and requires no further gateway access.
3. Construct an action context matching an existing interactive post, e.g., `{"action_id": "approve", "post_id": "<target_post_id>", "channel_id": "<target_channel_id>"}`.
4. Compute the forged token: `_token = HMAC-SHA256(derivedSecret, JSON.stringify(sortedContext))`.
5. POST a crafted payload to `http://<gateway-host>:<gatewayPort>/mattermost/interactions/<accountId>` with the forged `_token` field.
6. `verifyInteractionToken` at `interactions.ts:165-175` passes; the handler dispatches `core.system.enqueueSystemEvent` to the agent as if a legitimate user clicked an approved button.

## Validation Evidence
- Command: `pnpm exec oxlint src/ && pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test`
- Status: passed (with pre-existing baseline failures)

## Risk and Compatibility
- non-breaking; no known regression impact

## AI-Assisted Disclosure
- AI-assisted: yes
- Model: github-copilot/claude-sonnet-4.6

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Mattermost interaction tokens should no longer be forgeable from knowledge of the bot token; the branch now uses a generated or configured interaction secret instead of the old deterministic `HMAC-SHA256("openclaw-mattermost-interactions", botToken)` derivation.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on the rebased PR head `e261bcfab6de7ff05d18d2493ea60aedf234498d`, running the real Mattermost interaction module through Node with `tsx`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx --input-type=module -e 'import { createHmac } from "node:crypto"; import { ensureInteractionSecret, generateInteractionToken, verifyInteractionToken } from "./extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/interactions.ts"; const botToken = "redacted-bot-token-for-proof"; const oldDerivedSecret = createHmac("sha256", "openclaw-mattermost-interactions").update(botToken).digest("hex"); const generatedSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("proof-account"); const configuredSecret = ensureInteractionSecret("configured-account", "configured-proof-secret"); const context = { action_id: "approve", proof: "after-fix" }; const validToken = generateInteractionToken(context, "proof-account"); const oldForgedToken = createHmac("sha256", oldDerivedSecret).update(JSON.stringify(context)).digest("hex"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ generatedSecretLength: generatedSecret.length, generatedSecretIsHex: /^[0-9a-f]{64}$/.test(generatedSecret), generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation: generatedSecret === oldDerivedSecret, configuredSecretPreserved: configuredSecret === "configured-proof-secret", generatedTokenVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, validToken, "proof-account"), oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies: verifyInteractionToken(context, oldForgedToken, "proof-account") }, null, 2));'
```

- Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "generatedSecretLength": 64,
  "generatedSecretIsHex": true,
  "generatedSecretMatchesOldBotTokenDerivation": false,
  "configuredSecretPreserved": true,
  "generatedTokenVerifies": true,
  "oldBotTokenDerivedForgeryVerifies": false
}
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated interaction secret is random-looking 64-character hex and does not equal the old bot-token-derived secret. A token generated with the new per-account secret verifies, while a token forged from the old bot-token-derived secret does not verify.
- What was not tested: Full live Mattermost server button-click delivery was not exercised in this local proof; focused local runtime proof and Mattermost extension tests were run for the changed interaction-secret behavior.
